g.data-narration: Traditional project management follows a sequential linear approach where each phase must complete before the next begins.
Modern project management uses adaptive iterative approaches that embrace change and continuous customer feedback.
The Waterfall model has six distinct phases: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, and Maintenance.
Traditional approach emphasizes extensive documentation, rigorous change control, predictive planning, and milestone-based tracking.
The Agile Manifesto (2001) defines four core values: individuals over processes, working software over documentation, collaboration over negotiation, and responding to change over plans.
Scrum is an Agile framework using time-boxed sprints (2-4 weeks) with three key roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team.
Kanban is a visual workflow method emphasizing continuous delivery without rigid time boxes, using a board with columns like To Do, In Progress, and Done.
g.text: Key: Continuous flow • No time boxes • WIP limits • Visual workflow

g.data-narration: Extreme Programming (XP) emphasizes technical excellence through practices like pair programming, test-driven development, and continuous integration.
For a banking application, Waterfall takes 22 months with sequential phases while Agile delivers working software every 2 weeks through sprints.
g.text: Requirements (3 mo) → Design (3 mo) → Implementation (12 mo) → Testing (3 mo) → Deploy
Sprint 1 (2 wk): Account creation → Sprint 2 (2 wk): Transactions → Sprint 3: Reports...

g.data-narration: When requirements change mid-project, Waterfall requires formal approval and costs 10-50% more, while Agile simply reprioritizes the backlog in the next sprint.
g.text: Change Control Board evaluates → Impact analysis → Formal approval (weeks)
Product Owner adds to backlog → Prioritize in Sprint Planning → Next sprint

g.data-narration: Traditional risk management is upfront with a risk register, while Agile uses continuous assessment with small batch sizes limiting risk impact.
g.text: Identified in planning → Risk register → Mitigation plans → Reactive approach
Continuous assessment → Regular retrospectives → Small batch limits impact
